title: French polishing a small surface
subtitle: Charge the rubber, build shellac in thin circulars, pull off the oil
excerpt: How to apply a French polish finish to a small piece of furniture using a shellac-charged polishing rubber: making the rubber, bodying up the surface in thin coats, and pulling off the oil for a clear finish.
sourceNotes: Charles Hayward Cabinet Making and Finishing. W.A. Lincoln Complete Manual of Wood Finishing. Traditional finishing guides (public domain).

content.text: French polishing builds a high-gloss film of
shellac

in many thin layers rather than one or two thick coats. The technique rewards patience: each pass deposits only a fraction of a millimetre. Work in a warm, dust-free room; cold and damp both affect the finish.
Mixing the shellac

Dissolve dewaxed shellac flakes in methylated spirit at a ratio of approximately 1 part flakes to 7 to 8 parts methylated spirit by weight. Allow to dissolve fully (2 hours minimum, or overnight). The resulting solution should be thin enough to show a faint amber tint. Do not use pre-mixed polish that has been stored for more than six months: old shellac does not form a hard film.
Making and charging the rubber
Form a ball of cotton wool wadding about 50 mm in diameter. Fold a square of clean, lint-free linen around the wadding to form the
polishing rubber

, gathering the excess cloth at the back and twisting to form a handle. Pour a small amount of shellac into the wadding; the cloth face should feel damp but should not drip when pressed on a test surface.
Step 1, grain-fill the surface

On open-grained wood, work the rubber in tight circles across the surface to force shellac into the grain pores. This is the filling stage. Use short circular movements, keeping the rubber moving at all times: stopping causes a ridge. Allow to dry for 20 minutes, then cut back lightly with 240-grit sandpaper wrapped around a sanding block. Remove all dust with a clean dry cloth.
Step 2, body up

Charge the rubber and work it in figure-eight and circular patterns across the surface, adding tiny amounts of linseed oil to the cloth face (one drop per session) to prevent the rubber from sticking. Each pass should be light and gliding. Allow 15 to 20 minutes between sessions. After three or four sessions the surface will develop a film and the grain will be filled. If the rubber starts to drag, the shellac content is too high: untwist the cloth and let the wadding breathe slightly before continuing.
Step 3, cut back and spirit off

Once the surface is fully built, cut back gently with 000 steel wool to remove any dust or roughness. Wipe off the dust. Charge the rubber lightly with methylated spirit only (no shellac) and make three or four light strokes in the direction of the grain: this evaporates the oil residue left from the bodying stage and leaves a clear, bright finish. This is called spiriting off.
Common mistakes
Stopping the rubber on the surface.
Always land and lift the rubber while it is in motion; stopping leaves a stuck mark in the film.
Too much oil.
More than a trace of oil prevents the shellac hardening and leaves a milky bloom. One drop of linseed oil per session is the maximum.
Rushing between sessions.
Shellac applied before the previous coat has hardened will pull the surface. 20 minutes minimum between each bodying session.
